summary: Gruber examines how Google and Apple Maps have handled Trump's executive order renaming the Gulf of Mexico to the Gulf of America. Both companies now show 'Gulf of America' to US users, 'Gulf of Mexico' to Mexican users, and 'Gulf of Mexico (Gulf of America)' to everyone else worldwide. Gruber argues this approach is needlessly obsequious — the better policy would show the new name only to Americans (with the old name in parentheses) and leave the rest of the world seeing only 'Gulf of Mexico.' He draws parallels to Apple's capitulations to China's mapping demands and argues that showing the made-up name in parentheses to the entire world amounts to peddling propaganda. While acknowledging that completely ignoring the official US name change would be politically unrealistic, Gruber contends that both companies are letting fear of Trump's vindictiveness erode their institutional integrity.
key_points: Google and Apple Maps are regional services, not universal atlases — they already show different names and borders depending on user location, which makes this a policy decision, not a simple editorial choice.

The ideal realistic policy would be 'Gulf of America (Gulf of Mexico)' for US users only, with 'Gulf of Mexico' for everyone else — instead, both companies chose to broadcast the new name in parentheses worldwide, which legitimizes propaganda.
The renaming parallels Apple's capitulations to China on Taiwan labeling and inflated island sizes — the US is now on the same spectrum of authoritarian cartographic distortion.
The Associated Press has correctly maintained 'Gulf of Mexico' as its style, and the Trump administration's retaliatory ban of AP journalists reveals the insecurity behind the renaming.
Fear, not integrity or controversy-minimization, is driving both companies' decisions — they're squandering hard-earned reputations to avoid the wrath of a vindictive administration.
money_quotes: Trump has a lifelong history of putting his name on buildings he doesn't own. He'd rather have his name emblazoned on a building he doesn't own than own a building that doesn't bear his name. To Trump, the name on the sign is more important than the deed.
Shakespeare would have us believe that a sea by any other name would taste as salty. Trump doesn't read Shakespeare.
Now the United States is governed by another such dogma-driven regime.
Wrapping it in parentheses is like wrapping a dead fish in newspaper — it doesn't contain the stench.
Mockery is a powerfully subversive weapon against authority — even more effective, I'd argue, than a guitar.
Obsequiousness is inherently undignified. Google and Apple are proud companies. But both have chosen a gratuitously undignified presentation of this new American-dictated name to the entire world.
My take is that your willingness to accept this Gulf of America nonsense is an indicator of your willingness to accept much worse things coming from this administration.
Like Pinocchio turning into a real boy, Trump renaming the Gulf of Mexico is like a late night comedy joke turned real.
key_insight: Google and Apple's decision to show 'Gulf of America' in parentheses to the entire world outside Mexico reveals that fear of political retaliation, not cartographic integrity or controversy-minimization, is now driving how major tech companies respond to authoritarian edicts from the US government.
